straight_beat.name: Archie Ouellette
straight_beat.column:
The first time I saw a Blue Jays game, the team was still a fledgling franchise, and the parade in '93 was a celebration of a dream that hadn’t quite materialized. Joe Carter’s walk-off home run was a moment that stuck with me, not just because of the win, but because of the raw, unfiltered hope that came with it. Twenty-five years later, I still carry that same press pass, faded and frayed, but it’s a relic of a time when the Jays were a team on the rise. Today, they’re a team in the middle of a season that feels like it’s been stretched thin by inconsistency.  

Last night’s 10-4 win over the Twins was a reminder that this roster still

optimist.name: Margot Tremblay
optimist.column:
The loss yesterday was a gut punch, sure—but let’s not let it drown out the symphony of possibilities humming beneath the surface. Bo’s locked in, Kirk is framing like Pudge, and the Dome is going to shake like ‘93 before the leaves turn. Book it.  

You know the script: a mid-June stumble, a few games behind, and the chorus of “This is the year” starts echoing through the stands. Twenty years of April predictions, and here we are again, dancing on the edge of a playoff flame. The Twins took the cake yesterday, but that’s just the weather report—clouds clearing for a summer storm. The Jays’ lineup? A mosaic of grit and promise. Bo, with that cannon arm and a fastball that bends like a willow, isn’t just a reliever; he’s a heartbeat in the bullpen. Kirk, the kid with the hair and the heart, framing pitches like he’s got a map to the strike zone etched in his skull. These are the pieces.  

The front office? They’re not just building a team—they’re building a legacy. Trade deadlines, free-agent signings, and the quiet work of

pessimist.name: Doug Purvis
pessimist.column:
The Blue Jays lost again yesterday, but this time it felt different. Not because the Twins are suddenly a dynasty, but because Toronto’s inability to compete has reached a new level of absurdity. You’d think after seven games, a team with a 6-7 record would have figured out how to win. Instead, they’re still floundering, flailing, and flinging away opportunities like a kid with a baseball mitt and no clue where to throw.  

Let’s talk about the bullpen. It’s supposed to be the backbone of this team, right? The guys in the pen who keep the game close, who rally the crowd, who make you believe in the underdog. But yesterday, they looked like a group of guys who’d never held a baseball in their lives. The Twins scored four runs, sure, but Toronto’s relievers allowed three walks, two homers, and a double that should’ve been a single. It’s not just about the numbers—it’s about
